_meta.name: Goal Setting Frameworks
_meta.description: Frameworks for setting effective goals and creating action plans
_meta.use_when: Content includes goal setting exercises or action planning sections

smart_goals.name: SMART Goals

source.creator: George T. Doran
source.publication: There's a S.M.A.R.T. way to write management's goals and objectives

descriptions.professional: A mnemonic framework for goal setting that emphasises specificity, measurability, achievability, relevance and time boundedness. Originally developed for management contexts, the framework has been widely adopted across education, health, personal development and therapeutic settings. The criteria serve as a checklist to transform vague aspirations into concrete, actionable objectives.
descriptions.peer_led: SMART is a way to check if your goal is clear enough to actually achieve. A vague goal like get healthier is hard to act on. A SMART goal like walk for 20 minutes three times a week for the next month tells you exactly what to do, how to know if you did it, and by when. It takes fuzzy wishes and turns them into real plans.

background.origin: Doran created the framework in the context of management by objectives. The specific letters have been interpreted differently by different authors over time.
background.evidence_base: Goal setting research broadly supports the value of specific, challenging goals. The SMART framework is widely used though not always rigorously tested as a complete package.
background.why_it_matters: Provides a simple checklist to improve goal quality. Prevents common goal setting mistakes. Creates accountability through clarity.
background.limitations: Can feel mechanical or limiting for some. May not suit all goal types, particularly exploratory or learning goals. Sometimes overemphasises measurability at the expense of meaning.

criteria.name: Specific
criteria.question: What exactly will you do?
criteria.description: The goal clearly states what will be accomplished. Who, what, where and why are defined.
criteria.better_example: Go to the gym three times a week

criteria.name: Measurable
criteria.question: How will you know you have done it?
criteria.description: There is a clear way to track progress and know when the goal is achieved.
prompts: How will you know when you have achieved it?

criteria.name: Achievable
criteria.question: Is this realistic for you right now?
criteria.description: The goal is challenging but possible given current resources, skills and constraints.
criteria.poor_example: Run a marathon next week (never having run before)
prompts: Is this possible given your current situation?
What might get in the way?

criteria.name: Relevant
criteria.question: Does this matter to you? Does it connect to your values?
criteria.description: The goal aligns with broader objectives and values. It is worth doing.
criteria.poor_example: Learn guitar because everyone says I should
criteria.better_example: Learn guitar so I can play songs with my children
prompts: Why does this matter to you?
How does this connect to what you value?
Is this the right goal right now?

criteria.name: Time-bound
criteria.question: By when?
criteria.description: There is a clear deadline or timeframe for achievement.
prompts: What are the milestones along the way?

smart_goals.application_note: Works well combined with implementation intentions. After setting SMART goal, create if then plans for when and how to work on it.

woop.name: WOOP (Wish, Outcome, Obstacle, Plan)

source.creator: Gabriele Oettingen
source.publication: Rethinking Positive Thinking: Inside the New Science of Motivation

descriptions.professional: A four step mental contrasting strategy that combines positive visualisation of desired outcomes with realistic consideration of internal obstacles, followed by implementation intention formation. Research demonstrates that this approach outperforms either positive visualisation or obstacle focus alone. The method addresses the intention behaviour gap by anticipating barriers and pre programming responses.
descriptions.peer_led: WOOP is a way to turn wishes into plans that actually work. You start by thinking about what you want and imagining how good it would feel to achieve it. Then, instead of stopping there, you get realistic about what inside you might get in the way. Finally, you make a plan for what you will do when that obstacle shows up. Dreaming big works better when you also plan for the hard parts.

background.origin: Oettingen researched why positive thinking alone often fails to produce results. She found that mental contrasting, combining positive fantasy with obstacle awareness, was more effective than either alone.
background.evidence_base: Robust research support across multiple domains. Studies show effects on health behaviours, academic performance, relationships and professional goals.
background.why_it_matters: Addresses the gap between positive intentions and reality. Builds in obstacle planning from the start. More effective than positive thinking alone.
background.limitations: Requires honest self reflection about obstacles. May feel pessimistic to those attached to pure positive thinking. Works best for goals within personal control.

steps.name: Wish
steps.description: Identify a meaningful, challenging but achievable wish or goal
steps.guidance: Choose something that matters to you. It should be challenging enough to be exciting but realistic enough to be possible.
steps.prompt: What do you really want to achieve?

steps.name: Outcome
steps.description: Vividly imagine the best outcome of achieving your wish
steps.guidance: Close your eyes and really see it. How would you feel? What would be different? Let yourself experience the positive emotions.
steps.prompt: Imagine you have achieved this. What is the best thing about it? How does it feel?

steps.name: Obstacle
steps.description: Identify the main internal obstacle that might prevent success
steps.guidance: Be honest with yourself. What inside you, your thoughts, feelings, habits or beliefs, might get in the way? Focus on internal obstacles you can control.
steps.prompt: What is the main thing within you that could hold you back?

steps.name: Plan
steps.description: Create an if then plan to overcome the obstacle
steps.guidance: Use implementation intention format. When the obstacle appears, what will you do instead?

woop.application_note: The obstacle step is what makes this more effective than pure positive thinking. Do not skip or minimise it.

suggested_combinations.how: Use SMART to define the goal clearly. Use implementation intentions to create specific when then plans for action.
Use WOOP to explore motivation and obstacles. Use SMART to refine the goal into concrete terms.
WOOP already includes an implementation intention in the P step. Can add additional if then plans for other potential obstacles.
